perturbation.original_text: Evaluation Material shall not include, however, information which (i) is or becomes generally available to the public other than as a result of a disclosure by the Receiving Party or its Representatives in violation of this letter agreement; (ii) was available to the Receiving Party on a nonconfidential basis prior to its disclosure by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives; (iii) becomes available to the Receiving Party on a nonconfidential basis from a person other than the Disclosing Party or its Representatives who is not otherwise bound by a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives, or is otherwise not known to the Receiving Party to be under an obligation to the Disclosing Party or any of its Representatives not to transmit the information to the Receiving Party; or (iv) was independently developed by the Receiving Party without reference to or use of the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: Evaluation Material shall not include, however, information which (i) is or becomes generally available to the public; (ii) was available to the Receiving Party; (iii) becomes available to the Receiving Party; or (iv) was independently developed by the Receiving Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of 'Evaluation Material' included several exceptions related to confidentiality obligations and prior knowledge. By removing the phrases clarifying the conditions under which publicly available, previously known, or independently developed information is *not* considered Evaluation Material (specifically, those phrases referring to confidentiality agreements and obligations), the modified text creates ambiguity. It now implies that any information falling into those categories is automatically excluded, regardless of existing confidentiality agreements. This contradicts later sections that rely on the original, more nuanced definition to enforce confidentiality. For example, it contradicts the Receiving Party's obligations related to third-party confidential information provided to the Disclosing Party, thus creating an in-text contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_text: All information (i) about the Disclosing Party or (ii) about a third party (which information was provided to the Disclosing Party subject to a confidentiality agreement with such third party) furnished by the Disclosing Party or its Representatives (as defined below) to the Receiving Party or its Representatives, whether furnished before or after the date hereof in connection with the Proposed Transaction, and regardless of the manner in which it is furnished, is referred to in this letter agreement as "Evaluation Material."
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes critical qualifications from the definition of 'Evaluation Material,' specifically those pertaining to confidentiality obligations and prior knowledge. By removing the phrases clarifying when publicly available, previously known, or independently developed information is *not* considered Evaluation Material (those phrases referring to confidentiality agreements and obligations), the modified text implies that any information falling into those categories is automatically excluded, regardless of existing confidentiality agreements. This directly contradicts the original, more nuanced definition in the contradicted text, which includes third-party information provided under a confidentiality agreement with the Disclosing Party as 'Evaluation Material'. The changed text now makes it unclear whether this third-party information is subject to the Receiving Party's confidentiality obligations, thus creating an in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: For purposes of this letter agreement, (i) "Representative" shall mean, as to any person, its directors, officers, employees, agents and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, attorneys and accountants) and debt and equity financing sources and their advisors and Representatives (but shall not include any debt and equity financing sources that enter into a confidentiality agreement reasonably <PAGE> 2 acceptable to the Disclosing Party, which either names Disclosing Party as a third party beneficiary or to which Disclosing Party is made a party, and an executed copy of which is provided to Disclosing Party); and (ii) "person" shall be broadly interpreted to include, without limitation, any corporation, company, partnership, other entity or individual.
perturbation.changed_text: For purposes of this letter agreement, (i) "Representative" shall mean, as to any person, its directors, officers, employees, agents and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, attorneys and accountants) and debt and equity financing sources and their advisors and Representatives; and (ii) "person" shall be broadly interpreted to include any corporation, company, partnership, other entity or individual.
perturbation.explanation: By omitting the clause excluding debt and equity financing sources that enter into a specific type of confidentiality agreement from the definition of 'Representative', the modified text broadens the definition. This contradiction leads to uncertainty because debt and equity financing sources are now always considered 'Representatives' regardless of whether they have a confidentiality agreement in place with the Disclosing Party. This directly contradicts and impacts the 'Subject to the immediately succeeding paragraph, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party' section. The section relies on the original narrow definition of representatives to protect the disclosing party from information leaks.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the immediately succeeding paragraph, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party (i) except as required by law, rule or regulation, shall keep all Evaluation Material confidential, shall not disclose or reveal any Evaluation Material to any person other than its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in its evaluation of the Proposed Transaction or who otherwise need to know the Evaluation Material for the purpose of evaluating the Proposed Transaction and shall cause those persons to observe the terms of this letter agreement; (ii) shall not use Evaluation Material for any purpose other than in connection with its evaluation of the Proposed Transaction or the consummation of the Proposed Transaction in a manner that the Disclosing Party has approved; and (iii) except as required by law, rule or regulation, shall not disclose to any person (other than those of its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in its evaluation of the Proposed Transaction or who otherwise need to know for the purpose of evaluating the Proposed Transaction, which Representatives it shall cause to observe the terms of this agreement,) any information about the Proposed Transaction, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Evaluation Material has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The original text excludes specific debt and equity financing sources from the definition of 'Representative' if they have a confidentiality agreement. The changed text removes this exclusion, broadening the definition of 'Representative'. The contradicted text states that the Receiving Party shall only disclose Evaluation Material to its 'Representatives'. Because the definition of representative has changed, the Receiving party now has access to broader range of individuals. This directly contradicts the contradicted text which seeks to limit the disclosure of information, as it is reliant on the original narrow definition of representatives to protect the disclosing party from information leaks. Therefore, a strong contradiction exists.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this letter agreement by it and secondarily responsible for any breach of the terms of this letter agreement by its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall be responsible for any breach of the terms of this letter agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'and secondarily responsible for any breach of the terms of this letter agreement by its Representatives' eliminates the explicit assignment of secondary responsibility for the actions of its representatives. This weakens the Disclosing Party's protection and contradicts other sections of the agreement where the Receiving Party's control over its Representatives is assumed and relied upon for confidentiality. This particularly affects enforcement, as the Disclosing Party now has a more difficult path to pursue damages if a breach occurs due to a Representative's actions. The 'Subject to the immediately succeeding paragraph, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party' is weakened with this change.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the immediately succeeding paragraph, unless otherwise agreed to in writing by the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party (i) except as required by law, rule or regulation, shall keep all Evaluation Material confidential, shall not disclose or reveal any Evaluation Material to any person other than its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in its evaluation of the Proposed Transaction or who otherwise need to know the Evaluation Material for the purpose of evaluating the Proposed Transaction and shall cause those persons to observe the terms of this letter agreement; (ii) shall not use Evaluation Material for any purpose other than in connection with its evaluation of the Proposed Transaction or the consummation of the Proposed Transaction in a manner that the Disclosing Party has approved; and (iii) except as required by law, rule or regulation, shall not disclose to any person (other than those of its Representatives who are actively and directly participating in its evaluation of the Proposed Transaction or who otherwise need to know for the purpose of evaluating the Proposed Transaction, which Representatives it shall cause to observe the terms of this agreement,) any information about the Proposed Transaction, or the terms or conditions or any other facts relating thereto, including, without limitation, the fact that discussions are taking place with respect thereto or the status thereof, or the fact that Evaluation Material has been made available to the Receiving Party or its Representatives.
perturbation.justification: The original text held the Receiving Party secondarily responsible for breaches by its representatives. The changed text removes this secondary responsibility. The contradicted text states the Receiving Party 'shall cause those persons to observe the terms of this letter agreement', implying a responsibility for their actions. By removing the explicit responsibility for the Representatives' actions, the changed text contradicts the contradicted text, as it weakens the Receiving Party's obligation to ensure its Representatives comply with the agreement, thus creating a direct conflict.

perturbation.original_text: For a period (the "Restricted Period") commencing with the date of this letter agreement and ending on the earlier of (i) 15 months after the termination of discussions between the parties with respect to a Proposed Transaction and (ii) the occurrence of a "Significant Event" (as defined below), neither party hereto nor any of its Representatives shall, without the prior written consent of the other party or its board of directors or any committee thereof delegated the responsibility for such matters: (a) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any voting securities or direct or indirect rights to acquire any voting securities of the other party or any subsidiary of the other party, or of any successor to or person in control of the other party, or any material assets of the other party or any subsidiary or division of the other party or of any such successor or controlling person; (b) make, or in any way participate, directly or indirectly, in any "solicitation" of "proxies" to vote (as such terms are used in the rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC")), or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of any voting securities of the other party; (c) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit a proposal or offer (with or without conditions) in connection with any of the foregoing; (d) form, join or in any way participate in a "group" as defined in Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"), in connection with any of the foregoing; (e) otherwise act or seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or policies of the other party; (f) take any action that could reasonably be expected to require the other party to make a public announcement regarding the possibility of any of the events described in clauses (a) through (e) above; or
perturbation.changed_text: For a period commencing with the date of this letter agreement, neither party hereto nor any of its Representatives shall, without the prior written consent of the other party or its board of directors or any committee thereof delegated the responsibility for such matters: (a) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any voting securities or direct or indirect rights to acquire any voting securities of the other party or any subsidiary of the other party, or of any successor to or person in control of the other party, or any material assets of the other party or any subsidiary or division of the other party or of any such successor or controlling person; (b) make, or in any way participate, directly or indirectly, in any "solicitation" of "proxies" to vote (as such terms are used in the rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC")), or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of any voting securities of the other party; (c) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit a proposal or offer (with or without conditions) in connection with any of the foregoing; (d) form, join or in any way participate in a "group" as defined in Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"), in connection with any of the foregoing; (e) otherwise act or seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or policies of the other party; (f) take any action that could reasonably be expected to require the other party to make a public announcement regarding the possibility of any of the events described in clauses (a) through (e) above; or
perturbation.explanation: By removing the specific termination conditions for the 'Restricted Period' (i.e., 'ending on the earlier of (i) 15 months after the termination of discussions between the parties with respect to a Proposed Transaction and (ii) the occurrence of a "Significant Event" (as defined below)'), the modified text creates an indefinite restriction period. This contradicts the implied intent of the agreement, which is for the restrictions to be temporary and tied to the potential transaction. The lack of a defined end date makes the restrictive covenants overly broad and potentially unenforceable, as it places restrictions without limit which contradicts the implicit context of a potential deal.
perturbation.contradicted_text: For a period (the "Restricted Period") commencing with the date of this letter agreement and ending on the earlier of (i) 15 months after the termination of discussions between the parties with respect to a Proposed Transaction and (ii) the occurrence of a "Significant Event" (as defined below), neither party hereto nor any of its Representatives shall, without the prior written consent of the other party or its board of directors or any committee thereof delegated the responsibility for such matters: (a) acquire, offer to acquire, or agree to acquire, directly or indirectly, by purchase or otherwise, any voting securities or direct or indirect rights to acquire any voting securities of the other party or any subsidiary of the other party, or of any successor to or person in control of the other party, or any material assets of the other party or any subsidiary or division of the other party or of any such successor or controlling person; (b) make, or in any way participate, directly or indirectly, in any "solicitation" of "proxies" to vote (as such terms are used in the rules of the Securities and Exchange Commission (the "SEC")), or seek to advise or influence any person or entity with respect to the voting of any voting securities of the other party; (c) make any public announcement with respect to, or submit a proposal or offer (with or without conditions) in connection with any of the foregoing; (d) form, join or in any way participate in a "group" as defined in Section 13(d)(3) of the Securities Exchange Act of 1934, as amended (the "Exchange Act"), in connection with any of the foregoing; (e) otherwise act or seek to control or influence the management, Board of Directors or policies of the other party; (f) take any action that could reasonably be expected to require the other party to make a public announcement regarding the possibility of any of the events described in clauses (a) through (e) above; or
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the defined end date for the 'Restricted Period', resulting in an indefinite restriction. This directly contradicts the original intention and the contradicted text, which clearly states that the Restricted Period ends either 15 months after discussions terminate or upon a Significant Event. The omission creates a direct conflict and inconsistency, as the contradicted text explicitly defines the end of the period, while the changed text implies it continues indefinitely, making the agreement ambiguous and potentially unenforceable. This aligns with the 'Omissions - In Text Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: Subject to the terms and conditions of a definitive agreement regarding the Proposed Transaction and without prejudice thereto, each party hereto acknowledges that neither it nor its Representatives nor any of the officers, directors, employees, agents or controlling persons of such Representatives makes any express or implied representation or warranty as to the completeness of the Evaluation Material. The Receiving Party shall not be entitled to rely on the completeness of any Evaluation Material, but shall be entitled to rely solely on such representations and warranties regarding the completeness of the Evaluation Material as may be made to it in any definitive agreement relating to the Proposed Transaction, subject to the terms and conditions of such agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each party hereto acknowledges that neither it nor its Representatives nor any of the officers, directors, employees, agents or controlling persons of such Representatives makes any express or implied representation or warranty as to the completeness of the Evaluation Material. The Receiving Party shall not be entitled to rely on the completeness of any Evaluation Material, but shall be entitled to rely solely on such representations and warranties regarding the completeness of the Evaluation Material as may be made to it in any definitive agreement relating to the Proposed Transaction, subject to the terms and conditions of such agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'Subject to the terms and conditions of a definitive agreement regarding the Proposed Transaction and without prejudice thereto,' the text eliminates the conditionality of the disclaimer. This phrase originally indicated that the disclaimer regarding the completeness of Evaluation Material was only applicable if a final agreement wasn't in effect, thus acknowledging potential liabilities after an agreement has been established. Its removal creates an in-text contradiction, because the contract says they will only rely on such representations after an agreement, however by removing this the rely only on reps part will be valid before and after the agreement, making it contraditing and difficult to enforce. Thus the Receiving Party now can't be certain on the validity and completeness of the evaluation material that can cause the discloser to not even pursue an aggreement because they didn't even want to provide an accurated evaluation.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Subject to the terms and conditions of a definitive agreement regarding the Proposed Transaction and without prejudice thereto, each party hereto acknowledges that neither it nor its Representatives nor any of the officers, directors, employees, agents or controlling persons of such Representatives makes any express or implied representation or warranty as to the completeness of the Evaluation Material. The Receiving Party shall not be entitled to rely on the completeness of any Evaluation Material, but shall be entitled to rely solely on such representations and warranties regarding the completeness of the Evaluation Material as may be made to it in any definitive agreement relating to the Proposed Transaction, subject to the terms and conditions of such agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the Receiving Party's reliance on the completeness of the Evaluation Material is 'Subject to the terms and conditions of a definitive agreement'. The changed text removes this conditionality, implying that the Receiving Party cannot rely on the completeness of the Evaluation Material regardless of whether a definitive agreement is in place. The contradicted text is the same as the original text and still contains that condition, the changed text removing the subject to condition clause directly contradicts the contradicted text.
